data.title: Kyndryl Holdings Q4 Preview: A Lot Of Work To Restore Investor Confidence
data.description:
Kyndryl Holdings, Inc. (KD) is going to report its Q4 results on the 6th of May before the market opens, and seeing the whole drama unfolding, I wanted to go over what to expect and give some comments on what is going to be the most important part of the day.

What to Expect

The company is expected to report $0.47 and $0.26 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $3.75B in revenues. This translates to around -3% sequentially and around -1% compared to the same time last year. Additionally, there were 5 EPS downward revisions over the last 90 days.

There is not much growth here overall, and the question is, is the company going to beat or miss estimates in the upcoming report? If we look at the historic performance, we can see that the last year was not particularly great for KD. The last 4 quarters, KD missed on revenue estimates, as well as once on EPS.

I could see the company missing on both the top and bottom lines once again because the business itself is not doing particularly well since the spinoff from International Business Machines (IBM). The company used to report quarters over $4.5B, and now this number has dwindled to less than $4B.

For anyone interested in doing an options play, I would recommend writing options instead of gambling away on an out-of-the-money call or put. The reason is that most of the time, buying options results in losses in general, but in this situation, it is because the company's implied volatility is around 93%, while its historical volatility is closer to 48%. This means that there are traders who are pricing in uncertainty because something big is going to happen, and that is the company's earnings, and given that the volatility was already high due to the massive cratering earlier, earnings day will see a huge volatility crush. Implied volatility crush, or IV crush, is when prices of short and long options come significantly down as the massive event passes, and if the share price doesn't move enough in either direction, both sides end up losers. Here, the expected move is around 20%, so that means the market expects massive moves. You are better off writing options as far out as 20% under and over the price, and you will still get a good price, which will get crushed after the event, and you can buy back the contracts for cents and lock in your profits.

Here is what that may look like: 73% probability of profit, but if the move is well over and under 20% on the day, your max loss is $232.

This one is a bit too unpredictable, given what has happened with the company's shares recently and all the drama surrounding the filings and the departure of executives. I wouldn't be surprised to see swings well over 20%, and if that happens, I could see writers of options get burned, so I am not initiating anything here right now.

What I'm looking for

I believe the conference call will be dominated by the whole SEC situation and restatement of the last three reports. The call will be led by the CEO, Martin Schroeter, and the interim CFO, Harsh Chugh, so I am expecting to hear a lot of details regarding updates on remediation steps for disclosure controls and internal controls over financial reporting. I would like to hear how the management will show that the cash-management issue is contained and that the company is expected to file cleanly and on schedule going forward.

As I mentioned earlier, the last three reports were restated, but nothing had to be changed in terms of all the cash flows and other numbers in all of them, which is good to hear, but that didn't help its share price come back up. That is because investors don't just value correct numbers; they value trust. Trust in reporting to be done correctly, and trust that the future cash-flow quality is without flaws. The management will have to do a lot to prove to investors that this is not going to repeat and that everything has been duly fixed. The current discount is going to weigh on the company until all of these issues are addressed and resolved.

Besides that, the main issue I want to see is how the company is going to drive top-line growth going forward. I have to say it has been quite disappointing all these years. It needs to have a lot more hyperscaler and alliance-led deals to get its top line improved. Kyndryl Consult doesn't seem to be enough to help here, so I would like to hear how the management is going to go about expanding the segment and other higher-value advisory/implementation work to finally show us decent growth in sales, as well as profitability.

The management needs to win more accounts in cloud modernization, security, and, of course, AI and data, which is where all the high-quality opportunities lie currently. The company still deals with a lot of low-margin legacy contracts that it inherited from IBM, so I want to hear what the management is going to do about them. I need them to be more proactive here and renegotiate or get rid of them, which should improve profitability even before its top-line growth reaccelerates.

Should you buy Kyndryl before earnings?

I think the whole SEC drama will keep a lot of people away for now, and for a good reason. However, these things pass, and seeing that there was nothing that needed to be amended in terms of the company's financial performance, I could see that the reaction to the drama was overblown and its share price should recover; however, it may not happen that quickly because it will take time to regain investor confidence. Therefore, I am going to monitor this from the sidelines for now and check in on the day of the earnings to see what kind of move we get. This is quite exciting to witness.

data.title: Daily Journal Corporation: Maybe Not Enough Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Daily Journal Corporation (DJCO) is a quirky pick. A lot of people already know that Charlie Munger chaired the company and basically used it as a mini Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A) side project, initially buying out the LA Daily Journal and then turning it primarily into a holding company, but also another thing close to Charlie Munger's heart, which is a running a case management software business that is used in the legal industry, for filing things with agencies and courts digitally and other such services. The holdings in marketable securities cover quite a lot of the value of the company, which is sometimes a good start for a sum of the parts appeal.

The key things are the marketable securities and the Journal Technologies segment. The actual newspapers can mostly be ignored in the consideration of DJCO's value, as the vast majority of sales and profits would be from the court software. Our position is that the valuation looks about fair, there's nothing compelling an immediate buy from what we can see, especially given the market environment and exposure through marketable securities to cyclical sectors, mainly US banking. It's also an industry not without competitive threats, as we understand it from Tyler Technologies (TYL) in this legal vertical, which we also used as the comp in the valuation.

Results

The results in the latest disclosure were pretty good on the topline, despite a government shutdown that might have been a disruption risk to the client base consisting mainly of government agencies. Virtually everything grew except for consulting fees. Journal Technologies seems to be in the old-fashioned economics of selling perpetual licenses, and as opposed to subscription economics where all the services are bundled together in a blended periodic pricing, things are billed separately. Consulting fees would ordinarily be a lower margin segment and more ad hoc. The high renewal rate on multi-year contracts may have had something to do with the significant number of contracts secured before the end of the fiscal year, with the latest results being Q1. That they are declining YoY against a gain in licensing and maintenance, which is higher margin, should be contributing a solid mix effect.

This is being borne out in the reportable segment results. Journal technologies is seeing solid pickup in operating income. Other segment items, apparently effects from non-qualified compensation plans, hit the traditional (newspaper) segment to drive down overall operating profit gain.

Valuation

The valuation is below, with the marketable securities figure, which includes some unknown and unspecified holdings, coming from the latest 10-Q.

The EBIT figure is from Seeking Alpha, and the comp we took is from Tyler Technologies drawn from SA's TTM EV/EBIT multiple in the valuation section. TYL isn't particularly cheap according to SA's valuation grade within the general IT sector, a market that has traded down in part due to the SaaSpocalypse.

Daily Journal when valued according to the 10-Q marketable security value (concentrated mostly on US banks purchased during the post-financial crisis period, but which would have fallen YTD) and Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL) as a comp, it's trading around in line to Tyler Technologies — a 30 % upside according to our TTM model. Since it has a holding company character, and because of the low price of original purchase of a lot of the securities which means we'd expect capital gains tax, the true upside is much less than that. In the balance sheet, deferred income tax is $85 million. When we include that, the upside is almost halved.

That's before taking into consideration possible penalties for its holding company character. A 20% holding company discount is quite normal. Given that Tyler Technologies is a competitor in the field making acquisition inroads onto he courthouse market, and also is undergoing a shift to SaaS style subscription revenues (for a cloud based platform) instead of license plus maintenance revenues which are a set of economics that are often better appreciated by the markets, we feel the valuation of Daily Journal through comping is sufficiently generous. So without fussing too much further, we'd say the valuation is not especially strong on that relative basis.

Bottom Line

While growth is quite good in the operating business, most of the flexion in value comes from the marketable securities holdings, focused on Bank of America Corporation (BAC) and other Berkshire-esque banking picks. We don't think the general market and macro is particularly favorable at the moment with no end in sight for the Iran War, so we aren't that interested in betting on the movements of those securities. In terms of the upside from Daily Journal's discounted multiple relative to a peer like Tyler Technologies, we aren't sure there is enough margin of safety that cannot be explained by considerations such as theoretical realized tax gains and holding company penalties as are often applied by the market.

data.title: ValuEngine Weekly Market Summary And Commentary
data.description:
Weekly Market Recap - Week Ending April 24, 2026

U.S. equity markets delivered a mixed but constructive performance over the past week, with strength concentrated in technology and growth-oriented sectors while several cyclical and defensive segments lagged. Mega-cap tech and semiconductor-linked names continued to drive momentum, supported by strong investor appetite for AI and innovation themes, as reflected in the solid gains in technology ETFs. Meanwhile, sector dispersion remains elevated, with energy and select defensive areas showing resilience, while financials, communication services, and real estate faced pressure. This environment highlights an increasingly selective market backdrop, where leadership is narrowing and stock-specific performance is playing a more critical role in overall returns.

In the below tables, we use major ETFs as a proxy for some major indexes as well as each of the sector groups into which we divide the overall markets. Tracking these over time provides a more defined picture of the US markets than simply tracking major indexes. This is followed by notable individual stock movers over the past month, and finally, our full strategy outlook.

Strategy Note - Is a Stalemate Good for the US Stock Market?

Despite geopolitical friction, the U.S. stock market enters the final week of April 2026 at record highs, fueled by “Mag 7” earnings optimism and hopes for a diplomatic resolution to the U.S.-Iran stalemate. Markets are currently “shrugging off” the Iran conflict, operating under the assumption that the worst-case escalation is off the table. With some of the largest Mag 7 companies and other major brands due to report this week, investors are shifting their focus back to corporate fundamentals. While earnings are a massive focus, the Federal Reserve decision on April 30 will likely share center stage. In fact, 178 S&P 500 companies are scheduled to report this week, while more than 300 more companies from the iShares FTSE Russell 3000 Index ETF (IWV) also will be reporting.

Of the “Magnificent 7”, Alphabet (GOOGL) will be the first to report this week. The number is due out on Tuesday. GOOGL stock is rated 5 (Strong Buy) by our forecast model. Wednesday brings reports from Amazon (AMZN, rated 4 (Buy)), along with 3-rated (Hold) Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META). Apple (AAPL), reporting Thursday under new CEO John Ternus, is also rated 3.

Other major reporters this week include: Western Digital (WDC); Caterpillar (CAT); AbbVie (ABBV); Lilly (LLY); Exxon Mobil (XOM); Chevron (CVX); Visa (V); Mastercard (MA); Coca-Cola (KO); Qualcomm (QCOM); and Amgen (AMGN). WDC and CAT get our highest ranks. The rest of these stocks are all rated 3.

Beyond company reports, Thursday’s big watchful eye will be on the FOMC rate decision and Friday’s job report. One warning from a State Street Global Advisors strategist is that if peace talks in the Middle East gain momentum, Energy (XLE) is prone to a sharp reversal, while Tech (XLK) may see further gains as "war risk" premiums fade. XLK is rated 5, while XLE is rated 3. The sector with the lowest rating is Consumer Staples (XLP) with a rating of 1. However, that is assuming the market continues its gains. If the market should take a nosedive, portfolio managers will sell their most overvalued stocks. In that event, XLP has a good chance of outperforming by losing less than any other sector.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A Macro View This FOMC Week
data.description:
A look at the economy through the eyes of the bond market as FOMC meets

Fears about the war-driven rise in prices of certain commodities and their knock-on effects have bounced Treasury yields a bit. The 10-2 yield curve has flattened under this pressure as the market weighs whether or not this will manifest in a more hawkish Fed.

Interestingly, CME Group traders now hold a majority view of the Fed holding rates steady into June, 2027! I’ll believe that when I see it. Especially since we’ve come to view CME as little more than a wind sock for the direction the wind is blowing at any given time. Regardless, the bond market – through its most important feature, the yield curve – implies a firm Fed, Warsh or no Warsh.

Our view is that team Trump/Bessent/Warsh * is going to find a way to stimulate the markets into the mid-term elections. If that view proves correct, it does not have to come through public facing Fed Funds Rate policy. There is always the tool known as QE, a new round of which is early in its progression (see below), along with its close relative, Modern Monetary Theory.

Now let’s delve back into the economy and its unhealthy motor, debt expansion.

Economists call the rising GDP as measured in officially calculated inflation terms “real GDP”. It’s doing quite well.

Nominal GDP is obviously also rising even better over the very long-term. Conventional economists and market managers love these pictures, and swear by them. Gold bugs? Not so much.

Because even assuming the inflation figures are real, GDP is only “real” if you believe that debt can continually be expanded in service to propping GDP. If GDP is rising, the debt required to sustain it is rising even better. Fact.

Circling back to the point made above, while it certainly is possible, if not likely that CME is wrong again in its forward Fed Funds Rate projections, even assuming the view of rates being held firm for the next year is correct, the Fed can expand its balance sheet to support the economy and markets once again.

Indeed, they have already stated their intention to do so, and you can see total assets basing in preparation for a rise.

The percent change from a year ago makes it even clearer. We are on the verge of a stimulative phase.

While history has taught us not to fight the Fed, and indeed I don’t plan to, managing the situation is not as simple as doing what market players did for decades prior to the 2022 rupture of the long-term downtrend in the 30yr yield “Continuum”.

I realize I am not covering new ground here (where longer-term subscribers are concerned). But it bears review and repeating for myself, if not for you. The message of all of the above inputs is profound.

In my opinion, in the near-term the Fed is going to operate more or less as it had for decades into the end of the secular bull market in bonds in March, 2020 (which was confirmed with 2022’s long-term breakout in yields). I also believe that a vast majority of investors, especially money managers and financial advisers, will also operate under the old rules.

While a more broadly bullish back half of 2026 is anticipated, beyond that the new macro, featuring a secular rebellion in bonds, will favor hard assets, productive entities and resource-rich markets. Capital will have to go somewhere. But I expect it to flee debt leverage within markets overly financialized by decades of a now-defunct macro and its associated debt shenanigans, which have spurred what is now a new era of inflationary market signaling.

* Again, I expect Warsh to have strong input from Bessent, just as I believe Powell had former Treasury secretary Yellen whispering sweet “transitory” nothings in his ear when he should have been fighting inflation back in 2021.

data.title: Apollo Global: I'm Downgrading To Buy Ahead Of A Tougher Q1
data.description:
Thesis

Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO) is one of the largest alternative asset managers in the world, built around private credit, insurance-linked capital, and opportunistic private equity. The last time I covered this stock, I rated it a Strong Buy because the market seemed to be overlooking just how dependable its fee-based earnings really are. I also thought the market was placing too much weight on concerns about AI disrupting credit risk.

Since my call, the stock has gained about 8%, versus roughly 4% for the S&P 500 (SP500), a sign, just maybe, that the market is starting to recognize that disconnect.

Apollo continues to play a pivotal role in private-market capital flows, with the investment case still taking shape. With earnings set for May 6 pre-market and Apollo now tied to the €1.82 billion acquisition of Forvia’s interiors unit through Apollo-managed funds, there’s fresh ground to cover.

After going through the data, I remain bullish, with only a small downgrade. The long-term earnings engine is intact, but near-term uncertainty has increased.

Apollo’s Growth Story Faces an Early Test in 2026

Apollo already put out a warning before the official earnings report. They said their "alternatives" investment portfolio, think private deals, specialty lending, that kind of thing, only earned about 6% annualized in Q1 2026. That may sound fine at first, but management had guided investors to expect about 11% growth for the full year. In actual terms, that came out to about $205 million in pre-tax income. Part of the blame goes to lower income from one of their lending arms, ATLAS SP Partners. To be fair, the S&P 500 dropped about 17% annualized during the same quarter, so Apollo is basically saying that they held up better compared to the stock market. That's a defensible argument, but it doesn't close the gap between 6% and 11%.

So can their fee business pick up the slack? Apollo has a separate earnings engine called fee-related earnings, or FRE. Last year FRE grew more than 20%, and management guided for the same growth again in 2026. The core business model is still working: they make money by charging management fees on a pool of investments that continues to grow larger, they collect fees from investment deals they help arrange, and wealthy individual investors were still putting money into their funds at a strong pace before the markets became unstable. The bigger question is what the first quarter actually looked like for deal activity and investor inflows as tariff fears and market anxiety amped up. Apollo themselves described a moment in early April, which was called Liberation Day, as the single sharpest stress test the market faced all quarter. We should be able to find out on May 6 how that translated into actual fee revenue.

Their non-traded lending fund, ADS, is another thing to watch. ADS stands for Apollo Diversified Credit, and it's a type of fund that everyday wealthy investors can put money into, but it's less liquid than a normal stock or bond fund. Early 2026 brought more investors asking to take their money out than usual, across the whole industry. Apollo Global Management only partially met investor withdrawal requests, so investors did not get all their money at once. Management describes ADS as conservatively positioned, basically emphasizing senior secured first-lien loans, avoiding payment-in-kind debt, and maintaining low software-sector exposure. That's all true, but elevated withdrawal requests still hurt net inflows and create bad optics at a moment when private credit as a category is getting more scrutiny.

  • Transaction enterprise value: €1.82B
  • Sale multiple (IFRS adj. EBITDA): 3.1x
  • Sale multiple (U.S. GAAP proxy): 4.8x
  • FORVIA Interiors adjusted EBITDA (2025): €582M
  • Expected net debt reduction (seller): ≥ €1.0B
  • Expected gross debt reduction (seller): > €1.4B
  • Annual financial expense reduction (seller): €50M–€70M.

Meanwhile, Apollo just announced a new acquisition, a car parts company called Forvia Interiors. This is a carve-out deal, which means they are buying only one part of the company, not the entire company. Apollo Global Management already owns, or controls through funds managed by Apollo affiliates, several automotive businesses, including Tenneco, TI Fluid Systems (formerly TI Automotive), and Panasonic Automotive Systems. Together, these businesses bring in about $28 billion in yearly revenue and have more than 120,000 employees working in over 50 countries. The strategy on Forvia Interiors is that cabin design, premium materials, and in-car technology will keep advancing and consolidating. It’s a long-term thesis. This deal won't touch Q1 earnings at all. It closes in the second half of the year.

There's also a regulatory concern developing behind the scenes. The NAIC (National Association of Insurance Commissioners), which sets insurance rules in the U.S., rolled out new rules this year that could push Apollo Global Management’s insurance arm, Athene to hold more capital against the private credit assets on its books. And if that happens, margins on Athene’s spread income could get tighter. Those spreads flow straight into Apollo’s SRE, or spread-related earnings.

The bottom line heading into May 6: Apollo's full-year targets of more than 20% FRE growth and roughly 10% SRE growth are still officially on the table. But weak Q1 alternatives returns and a rough macro backdrop mean the second half of 2026 has to pick up the slack. Apollo has a decent track record of delivering on guidance (27% miss record; see FG stats above), which gives them some goodwill. But the preliminary numbers they already released make clear the year is no longer on an easy path, and the May 6 earnings call has to answer one core question: how exactly do they plan to make up the gap?

APO Looks Fairly Priced Ahead Of Q1

APO comes in at 14.48x earnings. Fair Graphs fair value benchmark suggests the stock should be trading at 15.30x earnings, which works out to an implied fair value of $130.31. So yes, the stock is technically cheap relative to that benchmark. But that gap is small enough that you shouldn't get too excited about it.

Meanwhile, analysts have been cutting their expectations. Over the last 90 days, Wall Street analysts revised their earnings estimates for Apollo 94% of the time in a downward direction. The reason is probably directly tied to Q1's alternatives portfolio only earning about 6% annualized, way below the 11% that management had baked into their full-year spread-related earnings guidance.

On the positive side, Apollo's revenue increased by more than 22% compared to the same period last year, and its operating cash flow rose by more than 120%.

But despite that strong growth, Apollo’s GAAP EPS dropped significantly from a year ago. And return on equity is getting worse, not better. So the company is growing fast at the top line but not translating that growth into better returns on the capital it's using.

On a forward P/E basis using non-GAAP earnings, Apollo trades more than 27% above the median company in its sector. Its price-to-book ratio sits at 3.25x. That's nearly 150% above what comparable peers trade at. That's not automatically a problem. And Apollo does have a credible long-term case. Analysts estimate EPS will grow at a 13.2% compound annual rate over the next three to five years. Apollo Global's higher valuation can make sense if its FRE keeps growing at more than 20% and its SRE recovers in the second half of the year.

The expected EPS growth rate of 13.2% per year over the next 3 to 5 years supports a solid long-term outlook. But for the stock’s current valuation to hold up, Apollo’s alternative investments portfolio needs to return to normal performance, and its deal and loan origination activity needs to stay strong despite, pardon the cliché, broader economic uncertainty.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, just a minor downgrade here to a Buy. Apollo has a business that earns recurring fees. And that part keeps getting bigger. It also has a large and very active lending and loan-origination business. On top of that, Apollo has an earnings engine tied to interest rate spreads, and that part of the business is expected to improve and recover over time. Those aren't going away. But at the moment, four factors are creating more near-term uncertainty than there was before: the Q1 alternatives portfolio earning 6%, almost every analyst on Wall Street cutting their estimates in the last 90 days, more investors than usual trying to pull money out of ADS, and new NAIC insurance regulations that could squeeze Athene's margins.

data.title: Public Storage: National Storage Deal Adds Upside, But Iran Risks Keep It A Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Public Storage (PSA), I rated them Hold, as the risk-reward seemed balanced given the potential for near-term macro headwinds while the company continued navigating the environment quite well.

Following a solid start to 2026 and the announced deal to acquire National Storage (NSA), PSA remains a Hold while their preferred stock is certainly worth looking into, with the common’s valuation unfortunately not reflecting a good enough margin of safety given the Iran-driven risk.

Latest Quarter

PSA started the year on a good note, with a beat on FFO and revenue in line with the market’s expectations, achieving an 18.3% increase in FFO YoY but an increase of only 0.2% in FAD and 0.3% on a per-share basis when adjusting for the effects of FOREX, stock-based compensation, and CAPEX, with the occupancy holding steady at 91.5%, while also announcing the appointment of Tom Boyle as CEO, effective April 1, 2026.

For 2026, PSA expects a drop of -3.9% to -0.5% in net operating income (mostly due to higher expenses), with $300 million worth of investments in development openings and a -3.7% to 0.2% growth in Core FFO YoY. They expect their interest expenses to rise significantly from $311 million in 2025 (and $297.9 million in 2024) to $349 million to $363 million in 2026, showing the impact of the current high-rate environment.

Financially, based on PSA’s latest report, we continue to see a solid position, with a net debt and preferred equity to EBITDA ratio of 4.1x. Recently, one of the company’s subsidiaries priced a $500 million 5% note offering due December 2035, highlighting their ability to access affordable capital even today alongside issuing stock, while PSA continues to hold a very solid A2 and A rating from Moody's and S&P.

PSA’s dividend stands at about 3.89%, representing a payout of about $2.11 billion, for a strong 75.14% payout ratio based on 2025’s FAD, which leaves plenty of room for improvements while also providing solid capital to focus on their balance sheet and growth.

To highlight the company’s multiple preferred shares again (from F to S), we continue to see them trading at a yield of around 6.2% to 6.3% now, which is in line with high-quality preferred stock such as ADC.PR.A, which also comes with a 6.20% yield today, but it’s important to note that preferreds like PSA.PR.N, PSA.PR.O, or PSA.PR.Q come with better conditions since they’re trading even more below their $25 liquidation preference and offer slightly better yields while also costing the company only 3.875% to 3.95% based on their initial yields.

In other words, since they are relatively cheap, there’s a very high chance to see these remain uncalled for a very, very long time, especially if interest rates remain elevated over a longer period, while they are covered well by the company’s earnings, rated A3 by Moody’s and BBB+ by S&P, serving as a very solid way to consider for investors wanting to park their money while waiting for better market conditions. Of course, there’s also the risk of seeing them trade at lower levels in a higher-for-longer rate environment, but I believe they’re among the stronger instruments if we’re talking about them from this perspective.

Macro Pressure Rises

Although they’re still doing well even today, it’s important to note that the company is far from immune to a weak environment, and there’s certainly plenty of potential to see that given the risks coming from the Iran conflict.

With the EIA expecting oil prices to remain at an average of $76 in 2027 (well above their expectations from a couple of months ago), it’s not hard to see how the environment can continue to deteriorate, with the region playing a crucial role in many key commodities that go well beyond the “simple” oil and gas, with these two being vital for plastics and fertilizers, for example, further highlighting how deep into the global economy this conflict can bite.

At this point, although we saw Iran proposing a truce that would imply reopening the Strait of Hormuz and later negotiating their nuclear capabilities, the damage that’s already been done to the region’s energy infrastructure, the potential impacts on the ongoing planting season, and the subsequent mix of weak supply (as repairs would be needed) combined with a boost in demand can elevate these commodity prices for longer while making an inflationary wave almost guaranteed, regardless of the status of the strait.

This can lead to two main scenarios at this point, with the worst one being a recession that would likely result in significant hits to PSA’s income given the effect on their clients, while the second one would imply a slower recovery for the economy as a whole. This is currently the main scenario seen by the IMF and S&P Global, with the latter expecting the Fed to delay rate cuts to 2027 as a result, translating into a higher-for-longer interest rate environment that can also be bad for PSA, affecting their borrowing costs alongside their tenants’ discretionary budgets.

Still, looking at it from a long-term perspective, the company should be able to benefit from trends such as urbanization, population growth, falling newbuilds supply, and even the shrinking size of living spaces due to affordability concerns, which inherently drive sustained demand for self-storage solutions offered by PSA and its peers.

Mordor Intelligence expects these and other trends to support a CAGR of 3.41% from 2026 to 2031, with North America remaining the largest market while still being significantly fragmented, which can be a major opportunity for PSA and peers (which manage roughly 15% of North American capacity as per the research firm’s data), allowing them to capitalize on their size and deliver solid growth over the long term.

In fact, that’s exactly what PSA is doing, recently announcing an all-stock deal to buy National Storage for an enterprise value of $10.5 billion, further enhancing their US exposure and expecting the FFO to grow by about $0.35 to $0.50 thanks to synergies over the first 3-4 years—while also adding even more high-yield preferreds under their name.

Risks Reiterated

Although we’re talking about an overall solid company that still has plenty of room to grow in the long term, we shouldn’t underestimate the market’s potential near-term focus, which can affect the company’s valuation.

Meanwhile, the business itself can be affected by the Iran-driven inflationary pressure that can delay rate cuts or even push the Fed into hiking rates if the environment gets bad enough to demand it, further pressuring REITs after an already long period of weakness.

Valuation

For PSA’s DCF to Equity valuation, taking into account the NSA acquisition and their guidance, I’m going to start from a combined FAD of $3.10 billion in 2026, which would grow at a solid 6% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to a mix of macro improvements that could boost their borrowing costs and occupancy rate, post-acquisition synergies, and their growth pipeline, followed by a 3.50% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, with a 2.50% terminal growth rate and a 9.50% discount rate to take into account the higher level of risk we see now due to the Iran conflict, as well as roughly 193 million shares outstanding after the acquisition.

This gets us to an equity value of $54.36 billion, for an intrinsic value of $281.66 per share, which is slightly below the current $305.48 at the moment of writing this, indicating that the market is likely willing to take a slightly lower margin of safety given their quality.

Conclusions

PSA remains rated Hold, starting to show the effects of a high-rate environment, albeit they remain in a strong financial position and should be able to continue taking advantage of the macro conditions regardless of the higher debt costs, although we shouldn’t ignore the rising near-term risks.

Meanwhile, their preferred stocks remain very interesting instruments to consider, offering a solid, high-yield way to park one’s money over the long term.

data.title: Village Farms International Deserves To Be Higher
data.description:
Back in early February of this year, I made the decision to rate Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF) a soft Buy. At the time, I found myself impressed with the overall growth story of the company. On top of this, we were seeing profitability improve, an attractive valuation, and a strong balance sheet all rolled into one. Investments being made by the company into international expansion, including in the Netherlands and Canada, would set the stage for further expansion. And when you added all of that together, the business made for an appealing prospect.

Unfortunately, since then, the stock has dropped 11.6%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 3.7%. By the way I view it, this move lower, which came in response to management falling short of analysts’ expectations when it announced results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, was an overreaction. And when you step away and look at the big picture, the company is certainly appealing enough to justify the bullish assessment that I detailed in that article.

Taking a fresh look at Village Farms International

At its core, Village Farms International operates these days as a player in the cannabis industry. The company does this through more than 8 million square feet of greenhouse space spread across parts of Canada and Texas and through partner greenhouses that are located in Canada and Mexico. And of course, the company has other operations as well. This has historically included growing certain crops like tomatoes, bell peppers, and cucumbers.

In my previous article, I detailed how the company's opportunity when it comes to the cannabis market will likely be more significant in Canada and the Netherlands than it is in the U.S. I cited one source that indicated that, in 2024, 54% of the U.S. population lived in the states where cannabis is legal for recreational purposes. And states representing 74% of the population of the country have it legal for medicinal purposes. With a majority of the U.S. already pro-cannabis, the potential for additional significant expansion here is modest. But it's not nonexistent.

With a majority of American adults already in favor of nationwide cannabis legalization, it's not surprising to me that we would eventually move in that direction as a nation. What is surprising to me is that President Donald Trump is making big moves in that regard. I say that only because, historically speaking, Republicans have been more resistant to the idea of cannabis being made legal. It has long been viewed, controversially of course, as a gateway drug. And with their history of the war on drugs, they would be the party least likely to make this change. But cursed we seem to be to live in interesting times.

On April 23rd of this year, his acting attorney general, Todd Blanche, signed an order to reclassify state-licensed medical marijuana as a less than dangerous drug. To be clear, this policy change does not legalize cannabis for recreational or medical use under federal law. All it does is make some other changes, such as allowing licensed medical cannabis businesses to receive tax breaks and removing a lot of roadblocks for researchers that would like to study the drug’s effects. Even though this does not make cannabis legal across the nation, it will make it easier for us to move in that direction as time goes on. And for companies like Village Farms International, this is conceptually a positive.

Recent financial performance achieved by Village Farms International has already been impressive. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the most recent fiscal quarter for the business compared to the same time last year. You can also see results for 2024 and 2025 as a whole. Revenue has improved, and profitability has turned from being negative to being positive. Cash flows for the company have largely increased year over year as well. This is great for the company. But it does require some additional context since not all parts of the company are performing nicely. Plus, we need to consider that while the latest quarter did see expansion on the top and bottom lines, revenue fell short of analysts’ expectations to the tune of $3.4 million, while the earnings per share of the company ended up being $0.01 lower than what analysts had hoped for.

In the chart above, you can see the main revenue engine for Village Farms International. This happens to be its Canadian Cannabis segment. Revenue jumped year over year for the final quarter from $34.2 million to $37.8 million. A lot of this expansion seems to have come from international export sales skyrocketing 384% at a time when retail branded sales were flat year over year. The company continues to boast the number one position in dried flour even though, according to management, they have been planning reductions in sales of lower-margin SKUs in order to focus on improving bottom-line results.

And when it comes specifically to the international export side of the business, the company is benefiting from certain major investments. Last year, for instance, the company spent $6.6 million in order to support its Canadian Cannabis segment, as well as its operations in the US and its Produce business. When it comes to its operations in Canada, the company recently mentioned that they began cultivation in the first half of its Delta 2 expansion project, which will allow the company to harvest an incremental 15 tons of production capacity for the remainder of this year. It also helps that the company is working on certain cost productivity improvements and transitioning toward a higher mix of higher-margin sales. That has been instrumental in boosting profitability for the segment.

This is not the only area of growth for the business. In the chart above, you can see that the firm went from generating no revenue in the Netherlands in 2024 to generating $3.3 million in the final quarter of last year. This is the successful launch of its first facility there. And according to management, it continues to operate at full capacity, and the company is in the process of scaling its local operating team in order to support Phase II of its growth plan. Once fully ramped up, the firm anticipates an annual production capacity of about 10 million tons in that country. And with the company describing the pricing environment there as "very strong," there is the opportunity for revenue and profit margins to come in strong.

It is worth noting that, due to lower prices and the impact of the privatization of some of its assets, its Produce segment reported declining revenue year-over-year, and with that, profitability also took a hit. A decline in sales from $6.1 million in the final quarter of 2024 to $4.8 million last year translated to a decline in profits from $4.8 million to negative $0.5 million. Weakness existed for the year as a whole.

Even though I mentioned at the start of this article that there is some opportunity for growth in the U.S., that is so far a rather small market for the business. In fact, during the final quarter of 2025, revenue in the U.S. dropped to $3.4 million compared to the $4.6 million reported a year earlier. And for the year as a whole, the $14.4 million generated from the U.S. fell short of the $17.4 million reported the previous year. Even though cannabis has been moving toward a more favorable regulatory environment, management mentioned that in eight additional states, they saw new restrictions on sales. This began in July of 2024. The company also saw a reduction in direct-to-consumer sales thanks to the proliferation of unregulated hemp-derived products in the U.S. market. So that is definitely a pain point for the company in the near term. This does show that the growth opportunity in the U.S. will be slower and will take longer than what many likely anticipated, even in light of recent regulatory changes.

In the chart below, you can see how the stock is currently priced using results from 2024 and 2025. The huge surge in profitability that the company experienced pushed the stock from being drastically overpriced to trading on the cheap. But in addition to being attractively priced, the firm also has a rock-solid balance sheet. After factoring in debt of $33.7 million, the business has a net cash position of $52.6 million. For an enterprise with a market capitalization of $326.9 million as of this writing, that is massive. And it means that the risk profile for the company in the near term, not to mention its opportunity to use that cash to grow, is meaningful.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I believe that the market is underestimating Village Farms International. I don't buy into the narrative that the cannabis industry in the U.S. is going to be a huge win for the company. But I do think that, even if that does not come to fruition, the stock is cheap enough and growth has been attractive enough, plus the balance sheet is strong enough, to justify a bullish outlook. That is why I have no problem maintaining the company as a Buy candidate right now.

data.title: Summers Value Partners Q1 2026 Partner Letter
data.description:
Dear Fellow Partners:

The Summers Value Fund LP (“the Fund”) returned -2.7% net 1 in the first quarter of 2026, underperforming the Russell 2000 Index ETF (IWM), which returned .9%, and the Russell 2000 Value Index ETF (IWN), which returned 4.9%. Since inception, the Fund has generated an annualized return of 11.2% net 1 compared to 6.8% for the Russell 2000 Index ETF and 6.8% for the Russell 2000 Value Index ETF.

Trailing Period Returns Summers Russell 2000 Russell 2000
Value Fund LP 1 Small-Cap Index 2 Value Index 3
YTD -2.7% 0.9% 4.9%
1 Year 16.9% 25.6% 27.8%
3 Years 56.0% 43.9% 46.3%
5 Years 57.7% 19.6% 31.1%
Cumulative Since Inception 4 130.0% 67.0% 67.1%
Annualized Since Inception 4 11.2% 6.8% 6.8%

Fund Commentary

The largest contributors to the Fund’s performance in the first quarter included Liquidia (LQDA), Avanos Medical (AVNS) and Consensus Cloud Solutions (CCSI). Liquidia’s launch of Yutrepia continues to outpace investor expectations, a trend we expect will continue. Consensus reported strong fourth quarter results, beating on revenue, profits and cash flow. Positive operating results were weighed down by negative investor sentiment towards the software industry. The largest detractors were Electromed (ELMD) and Embecta (EMBC). Electromed declined despite a strong financial update in February - an unexpected reaction. Embecta sold off after narrowing its fiscal 2026 guidance to the low end of the range. We added to our position on this weakness.

The Fund ended the quarter with nine long positions and no short positions. During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Theravance (TBPH) and exited ADMA Biologics (AMDA), as it fell short of our expectations, and we identified more attractive opportunities for our capital. We also covered our lone short position for a modest gain.

Our top five holdings at quarter-end were Liquidia, Electromed, Consensus Cloud Solutions, Avanos Medical and Embecta. We finished the quarter with approximately 5% in cash.

Position Updates

Avanos Medical (AVNS) - $1.25 billion market cap

Avanos was formerly the healthcare division of Kimberly-Clark before being spun off as a separate company in 2014. I first met the management team on the spin-off roadshow while at a prior firm. The company has been a tale of two businesses: a high-quality enteral feeding segment with a dominant global market position featuring mid-single-digit revenue growth and a double-digit operating margin, and a structurally challenged hospital pain segment with stagnant revenue and breakeven profitability. The lack of strategic fit between the two segments created a persistent overhang. Combined with years of operational missteps and poor capital allocation, the stock traded well below its spin price, reaching a valuation we rarely see in the medical device industry - less than 1x EV/Sales and ~7x normalized earnings.

Despite following the company for over a decade, we only initiated a position in July of last year. Our interest was driven by a leadership change announced earlier in the year. Dave Picitti, formerly head of Siemens Healthineers' U.S. business, was appointed CEO in March. Beyond his strong pedigree, his compensation package included performance-based shares that only vested at meaningfully higher share prices, creating clear alignment with shareholders. At the same time, the stock was trading at an all-time low with minimal investor expectations after years of disappointment - an ideal setup for our value-focused strategy.

On April 14, Avanos agreed to be acquired by American Industrial Partners for $25 per share in an all-cash transaction, representing a 72% premium. The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026. Our cost basis is $12.15, which represents a gain of 106% at the takeout price. We plan to hold a small residual position through closing to qualify for long-term capital gains treatment. Avanos is the seventh portfolio company to be acquired since inception. We expect M&A to remain an important contributor to the Fund's returns going forward.

Theravance (TBPH) - $924 million market cap

We recently joined the Yet Another Value Investor podcast to discuss our views on Theravance, a company we have followed for over a decade. Theravance markets Yupelri for COPD in partnership with Viatris. We initiated our position on March 3, after the company announced that its lead development program, ampreloxetine, had failed its pivotal phase 3 clinical trial. The same press release also outlined a major corporate restructuring, including a complete exit from R&D and a 50% reduction in G&A headcount. In total, management expected these actions to reduce annual operating expenses by $70 million. Beginning in the third quarter of 2026, Yupelri royalties, combined with interest income, are expected to generate $60-70 million of annual cash flow. With patent protection extending through April of 2039, Yupelri represents a long-duration and growing cash flow stream. Viatris reported 12% sales growth for Yupelri in 2025, and we expect continued growth driven by increased U.S. hospital adoption and the forthcoming launch in China.

The U.S. commercial business is the primary driver of value. Viatris and Theravance split Yupelri economics 65/35, with Theravance supporting a modest hospital-focused sales effort. We value the U.S. business at $520 million, assuming 2.5% revenue growth per year through 2038, and a 10% discount rate. In China, Yupelri was approved in June 2025, with launch expected by year-end 2026. Theravance will receive a 14-20% royalty, plus up to $37.5 million in milestones. Viatris' scale with over 4,000 sales reps should support a successful launch. We value the China opportunity at $200 million, assuming $350 million in peak sales by 2038, inclusive of $7.5 million of milestone payments. Theravance also holds $2.6 billion of Irish tax assets, which we value at $75 million. The company should have at least $500 million of cash by early 2027, including a final $100 million Trelegy milestone payment from GSK, which requires only 2% sales growth versus 2024 - a low hurdle.

The board formed a strategic review committee in November 2024, led former Royalty Pharma CFO Susannah Grey. The committee has already monetized the remaining Trelegy economic interest for $225 million and is now evaluating strategic alternatives, including a sale of the company. Viatris is the natural acquirer, though royalty-focused buyers may also be interested.

We estimate intrinsic value at $23 per share, or more than 60% upside from our cost basis of $13.80.

In Closing

I would like to express my sincere appreciation to those investors who added to their accounts this year. One of our greatest competitive advantages is our patient and long-term oriented investor base. The market environment is currently providing us with attractive investment opportunities. We believe the M&A theme in healthcare will be a tailwind that could benefit our portfolio for many years. Our strategy has ample capacity, and we welcome like-minded individuals to join the partnership. Interested parties may contact Alison Tomlinson at atomlinson@summersvalue.com .

Sincerely,

Andrew Summers, CFA

Managing Partner

Performance Disclosure

1 Summers Value Fund LP current year net return is unaudited. Net returns are based on the management fee and incentive allocation applicable to Class B Interests (1.25% management fee; 20% incentive fee above a 6% annual cumulative hurdle rate). Net return is not necessarily indicative of any single investor's performance. An investor's return may vary from the results shown based on different fee structures and fund-level expenses. Performance reflects the reinvestment of dividends and income. The performance information given is historic and should not be considered as an indication of future performance. 4 June 4, 2018

Definitions:

Indexes: The performance of market indexes is provided for the purpose of making general market data available as a point of reference only. These indexes are widely recognized by investors, followed by the investment industry and readily available to the investing public. The indexes are unmanaged and do not reflect fees and expenses associated with the active management of portfolios. The performance returns of the indexes were obtained from recognized statistical sources and include the reinvestment of dividends and income. Although Summers Value Partners LLC believes these sources to be reliable, it is not responsible for errors or omissions from these sources.

2 iShares Russell 2000 Index ETF (IWM): The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of approximately two thousand small-cap companies in the Russell 3000 Index, which is made up of 3,000 of the largest U.S. company stocks. This unmanaged index serves as a benchmark for U.S. small-cap stocks in the United States.

3 iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF (IWN): The Russell 2000 Value Index measures the performance of companies from the broadly diversified Russell 2000 universe that reflect value characteristics. This unmanaged index serves as a benchmark for U.S. small-cap value stocks in the United States.

Disclaimer:

The information and statistical data contained herein have been obtained from sources, which we believe to be reliable, but in no way are warranted by us to accuracy or completeness. We do not undertake to advise you as to any change in figures or our views. Past performance results are not a guarantee of future performance results.

This report includes candid statements and observations regarding investment strategies, individual securities, and economic and market conditions; however, there is no guarantee that these statements, opinions, or forecasts will prove to be correct. These comments may also include the expression of opinions that are speculative in nature and should not be relied on as statements of fact.

Summers Value Partners LLC is committed to communicating with our investment partners as candidly as possible because we believe our investors benefit from understanding our investment philosophy, investment process, stock selection methodology and investor temperament. Our views and opinions include "forward-looking statements" which may or may not be accurate over the long term. You should not place undue reliance on forward-looking statements, which are current as of the date this report was written. We disclaim any obligation to update or alter any forward-looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events or otherwise. While we believe we have a reasonable basis for our appraisals and we have confidence in our opinions, actual results may differ materially from those we anticipate.

The information provided in this material should not be considered a recommendation to buy, sell or hold any particular security.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Calix: Recent Pullback Drives Bullish Update
data.description:
Calix, Inc. (CALX) has seen its stock fall under some pressure after the company warned of potential margin pressure given the rising prices across the memory ecosystem. Of course, this is something investors should be cautious about, but the magnitude of the pullback seems to be overdone.

Calix is one of the leading providers of cloud and software solutions to broadband service providers, essentially enabling high-speed internet deliverability and the related managing services. The company also supports broadband partners who are looking to further expand into underserved rural areas, supporting fiber deployment projects.

The company has undergone a transition from a legacy telecom hardware vendor into a higher-margin, recurring-revenue platform business. Historically dependent on selling broadband access infrastructure, Calix has pivoted its focus towards an end-to-end provider of cloud software and managed services.

For investors, the prospect of stickier, recurring revenue is attractive, and this pivot has also pushed for a higher margin profile. As seen in the stock chart above, the stock price rapidly appreciated as the company demonstrated early signs of success during this model transition.

This past quarter was a bit of a challenge as gross margins are expected to come under pressure. However, I believe this is directly related to supply-chain challenges rather than internal operational difficulties.

My last published article on Calix was probably a little premature in calling the top, but the stock has essentially round-tripped back to the starting point. Thus, long-term investors should closely follow this pullback, as it presents a great buying opportunity.

Financial Update

During the company's recent Q1 results, the financial metrics were actually quite solid. Revenue grew 27% yoy to $280 million and was close to the high end of guidance. Gross margin—which has been the big indicator investors look out for—came in at a healthy 57.2%. Ultimately, this led to non-GAAP EPS of $0.40, at the high end of the previously provided guidance range.

Although the financial results came within the guidance range, the challenge has been around the trajectory of gross margins. With the company still on its model transition journey towards higher-margin subscription revenue, it is of the utmost importance for the trajectory of margins to remain positive. However, this has not been the case before, with gross margins declining sequentially over the past four quarters.

Management has discussed one of the major reasons behind this pressure.

The sequential and year-over-year decreases related primarily to the transition from our second-generation platform to our third-generation platform during which time we operated in a dual cloud environment to successfully support customer migrations. We substantially completed this transition in the first quarter with no material impacts expected from dual clouds on our future quarterly results.

However, guidance for Q2 includes a gross margin of 54.25-57.25%, which would represent another sequential decline compared to the Q1 margin of 57.2%. Now, this is where investing in a model transition story is tricky.

At face value, the negative trajectory is concerning, and investors have already started to question the true profitability profile of the new subscription revenue. Yes, there are external factors impacting this, including higher memory costs (input).

Our gross margin guidance for the second quarter of 2026 is between 54.25% and 57.25%. Reflecting the effects of higher memory component costs, the impact from surcharges and the customer and product mix. The decline in appliance gross margin is expected to be offset by improvement in software and service gross margin as the dual clog costs abate, and we optimize the current cloud environment. For the year, we expect our non-GAAP gross margin to decline between 50 and 150 basis points.

I believe over time, the cyclicality of memory prices will revert to the mean. But in the meantime, Calix will be on the hook for absorbing much of these costs, as only so much can be passed through to customers.

Just a few days after the company's earnings report, Calix hosted an investor day, including the unveiling of its target financial model. As seen above, the company expects non-GAAP gross margin to improve by +100-200bps per year starting in 2027, essentially acknowledging the ongoing headwind in 2026. With OpEx expected to grow materially slower than revenue, this should set up for very strong margin expansion and EPS growth over the coming years.

In my opinion, the company's margin target is achievable. For starters, memory prices have rapidly increased, with 80-90% price increases for DRAM and NAND already in 2026. The extreme price increase makes it incrementally challenging for Calix to pass through to customers. However, over a longer time horizon, even if prices stay high, Calix will be able to pass through some of these expenses.

Probably more important to the margin trajectory is the company's ongoing model transition to subscription revenue. This mix shift will naturally drive higher gross margins. Even if memory prices continue on this extraordinary increase, Calix's ability to drive more subscription revenue will inherently drive margins higher.

Right now, the focus is much more on whether or not the trajectory of gross margin can improve. I am a believer that it will, and as discussed more below, this could result in material EPS growth over the next few years.

Valuation

With Calix not 100% a software company, valuation should be looked at as some sort of earnings multiple. The model transition can make a pure revenue multiple less relevant and potentially distort what's going on under the hood.

With the stock trading at ~24.4x forward EPS, it is difficult to argue this is a cheap buying opportunity. Add in concerns around the near-term margins and input costs; the downside could be well below 20x forward earnings.

According to Yahoo Finance, consensus currently expects 2026 EPS of $1.78.

Using the company's target financial model from above, 15%+ revenue growth combined with outsized margin expansion could easily result in ~25%+ EPS CAGR through 2028. Using this back-of-the-envelope math, this would result in a 2028 EPS of ~$2.80. In a downside scenario, this would probably be closer to $2.25, and an upside scenario could be closer to $3.50.

Even when assuming ongoing multiple contraction, 20x a projected 2028 downside EPS of $2.25 would still be a $45 stock, implying a flat stock over the next few years. Right now, I believe the market is pricing in something much closer to this scenario rather than a bullish outcome.

In a bullish scenario and assuming the multiple stays at 24x, this would result in a stock price of ~$84, or nearly doubling over the next two years. Yes, this is highly optimistic, but in my opinion, this quick math shows a positive downside-upside scenario.

Risks

The biggest risk to my bullish thesis is that memory prices remain high and Calix's gross margins continue to compress. If the current trajectory of gross margin continues, investors would become very hesitant to buy any weakness, and shares are likely to fall much lower.

Additionally, the company is still correlated to hardware cycles, which are typically cyclical and volatile. Yes, Calix is pivoting to a more software subscription model, but this transition is still ongoing.

Finally, Calix competes against much larger incumbent vendors, including Ciena Corporation (CIEN), Extreme Networks, Inc. (EXTR), and Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO). These vendors may have more financial flexibility to absorb the higher input costs, which ultimately could lead to pricing pressure.

Final Thoughts

The recent pullback in shares appears to be somewhat warranted given the gross margin pressure, macro uncertainty, and general concern around how AI will impact the future demand. Investors are rightfully concerned about these factors, and the stock has pulled to levels last seen over a year ago. Much of this has more to do with investor sentiment (lower multiple) rather than operational performance (positive earnings).

However, the recent pullback seems a bit overdone, and management laid out a confident roadmap, including a multi-year target financial framework. If Calix can start to achieve positive gross margin progression and demonstrate its ability to navigate this complicated supply chain, the stock appears to have much more upside potential than downside risk.

data.title: abrdn Ultra Short Municipal Income Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The abrdn Ultra Short Municipal Income Fund (Institutional class shares, net of fees)(ATOIX) returned 0.57% for the first quarter and underperformed the 0.64% return of its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond 1 Year (1-2) Index.1

During the quarter, allocations to multi-family housing detracted from relative performance. Conversely, the Fund's exposures to other revenue, industrial and hospitals bonds were favorable.

In terms of credit quality, AA-rated bonds detracted slightly, while BBB and A-rated bonds improved relative performance.

Market review

The overall municipal (muni) bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, returned -0.18% for the first quarter of 2026. The short end of the muni market outperformed both the intermediate and long end of the market, with the Bloomberg Municipal 1-2 Year Index returning 0.64%, the Bloomberg Municipal 4-6 Year Index returning 0.01%, and the Bloomberg Municipal 17-22 Year Index returning 0.37%. The taxable muni bond market experienced positive performance over the quarter, with the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index returning 0.43% over the period, which compares favorably to the negative performance of the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index, which returned -0.05% over the same period. Meanwhile, high yield muni bonds outperformed their corporate counterparts, with the Bloomberg Municipal Bond: High Yield Index returning 0.71% over the quarter compared to a -0.50% return of the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index.

Total Returns (as of 03/31/26)

5 Years (p.a.) 2.24 2.45 1.82
3 Years (p.a.) 3.22 3.48 3.04
1 Year 2.60 2.86 3.04
Year to Date 0.51 0.57 0.64

1 month 0.19 0.11 -0.33

PAST PERFORMANCE DOES NOT GUARANTEE FUTURE RESULTS.

The performance data quoted represents past performance and current returns may be lower or higher. Class A shares have a 0.25% 12b-1 fee. The investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. To obtain performance information current to the most recent month-end, which may be higher or lower than the performance shown above, please call 866-667-9231 or go to aberdeeninvestments.com/us/literature.

Returns prior May 7, 2018 reflect the performance of a predecessor fund. Please consult the Fund's prospectus for more detail.

Total returns may reflect a waiver of part of the Fund's fees for certain periods since inception, without which returns would have been lower. Expenses stated as of the Fund's most recent prospectus. All classes of the Fund have contractual waivers in place and may not be terminated before February 28, 2027 without approval of the Independent Trustees.

1 The Bloomberg Barclays Municipal Bond: 1 Year (1-2) Index is an unmanaged index that includes bonds with a minimum credit rating of BAA3, are issued as part of a deal of at least $50 million, have an amount outstanding of at least $5 million, and have maturities of 1 to 2 years. Indexes are unmanaged and have been provided for comparison purposes only. No fees or expenses are reflected. You cannot invest directly in an index. Index performance is not an indication of the performance of the Fund itself.

For current holdings information, please visit abrdn Ultra Short Municipal Income Fund – Portfolio Holdings

Annualised GDP grew by a lower-than-expected 0.7% in the fourth quarter, compared to the preliminary estimate's 1.4% and the 4.4% expansion recorded in the third quarter. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) held the federal funds target range at 3.50-3.75% amid an uncertain macroeconomic backdrop. The yield spread between two- and 10-year Treasuries decreased as the curve modestly flattened, beginning the quarter at 69 basis points (bps) and ending the quarter at 52 bps.

Activity

Over the quarter, we focused on maintaining the Fund's duration through investments in short-term bonds and variable-rate securities. We continued to find good relative value for income-focused investors in these instruments as the front end of the yield curve remains inverted. The allocation of capital helped to bolster the Fund's yield as well as provide stability in the net asset value (NAV).

Outlook & strategy

As we move into the second quarter of 2026, we maintain a constructive outlook for the muni bond market, supported by strong fundamentals and gradually improving technical factors. While the yield curve has normalized modestly from prior inversions, we remain cautious about meaningfully extending duration as the opportunity for yield pick-up through extension remains limited and policy uncertainty continues to cloud the rate outlook.

Muni credit fundamentals remain solid. Tax collections continue to track favorably on a year-over-year basis, balance sheets are generally healthy, and reserve levels remain elevated across many issuers. In this environment, we believe muni bonds are well-positioned to navigate evolving interest-rate dynamics with relative stability and attractive tax-efficient income, particularly in the front end.

On the technical front, the muni market absorbed a record US$580 billion of issuance in 2025, supported by strong fund flows and reinvestment demand. Fund flows exceeded US$50 billion for the year, helping to offset elevated supply. Issuance in 2026 is expected to remain robust, with forecasts ranging from US$600-650 billion, driven by refunding activity and ongoing infrastructure investment needs. Issuance in the first quarter of 2026 totaled US$129.6 billion, up 7% year over year. While supply remains elevated, we believe demand from income-oriented investors should continue to provide support, particularly at the front end of the curve.

Against this backdrop, we are emphasizing credit fundamentals while selectively adding lower-rated names to capture attractive yields. We continue to favor issuers and sectors positioned to outperform in a potential economic slowdown. Maintaining the Fund's conservative duration positioning while focusing on credit fundamentals should result in a more stable NAV throughout an uncertain and volatile market landscape. With a relatively shorter duration over time, we feel the Fund should be insulated from sharp movements in its NAV relative to its peers.

Important information

Potential losses that may arise from changes in the market conditions which in turn affect the market prices of the investments of the Fund.

The Fund is subject to the risk that the Adviser or Subadviser may make poor security selections.

Fixed income securities fluctuate in price based on changes in an issuer's financial condition and overall market and economic conditions. The value of a fixed income security may also fall due to specific conditions that affect a particular sector of the securities market or a particular issuer. Fixed income securities are subject to, among other risks, credit risk, extension risk, issuer risk, interest rate risk, market risk and prepayment risk.

Municipal bonds can be significantly affected by political and economic changes, including inflation, as well as uncertainties in the municipal market related to taxation, legislative changes, or the rights of municipal security holders.

The amount of income received by the Fund will go up or down depending on day-to-day variations in short-term interest rates, and when interest rates are very low the Fund's expenses could absorb all or a significant portion of the Fund's income.

Investing in mutual funds involves risk, including possible loss of principal. There is no assurance that the investment objective of any fund will be achieved.

Standard & Poor's credit ratings are expressed as letter grades that range from "AAA" to "D" to communicate the agency's opinion of relative level of credit risk. Ratings from 'AA' to 'CCC' may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the major rating categories. The investment grade category is a rating from AAA to BBB-.
